EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 23W developed about 400 miles east-southeast of Guam on the morning of October 26th, and intensified into Tropical Storm Mirinae late that evening. Moving steadily west-northwest toward the Marianas, Tropical Storm Mirinae passed about 15 miles north-northeast of Rota about 1000 GST on the 28th with maximum sustained winds of 40 mph. ||In the Marianas, the only island significantly affected was Rota, where tropical storm conditions were experienced during the morning and early afternoon of the 27th. The automated observing station on Rota reported a maximum sustained wind of 36 mph from the northwest at 2251 GST, and the peak gust was from the south at 64 mph in the hour ending at 1251 GST. The minimum sea-level pressure was 995.5 mb at 1051 GST, and 3.38 inches of rain was recorded between 0400 and 1400 GST. ||No injuries or property damage were reported on Rota.

On October 15th, the automated station at Apra harbor, Guam, reported gusts of 59 mph from the east in the hour ending at 0751 GST, and 61 mph from the east-southeast in the hour ending at 1151 GST. These gusts were produced by brief lines of showers associated with the passage of minimal Tropical Storm Lupit well south of Guam. No damage was reported.

Tropical Depression 15W developed from a disturbance about 700 miles east of Guam on the 12th of September, and quickly intensified into Typhoon Choi-wan on the 13th. Moving steadily west-northwest toward the far northern Marianas, Choi-wan became a super typhoon just before passing directly over Alamagan Island at about 1600 GST on the afternoon of September 15th with maximum sustained winds of 150 mph.||On Alamagan, all six wood-and-tin residential structures were destroyed, and the 14 residents were left without food or water. Supplies were airlifted in by helicopter on the morning of the 17th, and a family of six, including a baby born on the 13th, was evacuated to Saipan for medical evaluation. One young girl sustained minor injuries. The remaining eight residents were evacuated on the 29th. Total damage is estimated at $25,000.||On Agrihan, about 75 miles north of Alamagan, winds peaking at an estimated 75 mph caused relatively minor damage to the two houses and to crops. The island's four residents were unharmed, and declined evacuation. Total damage is estimated at $5,000.

Tropical Depression 19W developed about 250 miles south of Guam early on the morning of September 28th, and intensified into Tropical Storm Parma late that evening as it moved west about 120 miles east of Fais Island in Yap State. After giving Fais a brief brush with minimal tropical storm conditions on the morning of the 29th, Parma turned west-southwest, passing about 100 miles south of Yap that evening. Parma then turned west-northwest again and passed directly over the island of Ngulu about 0500 GST the morning of the 30th with maximum sustained winds of 60 to 65 mph. By 1900 GST that evening, Parma had moved quickly away, and was in the Philippine Sea 250 miles northwest of Ngulu.||The only island to sustain any significant damage was Ngulu, where winds estimated at 60 to 65 mph were experienced. The roof of the community center was damaged by a fallen breadfruit tree, and four homes were damaged; mainly the thatched roofs. No crops were damaged, but about 40% of the banana and papaya trees sustained some damage. Total damage to buildings is estimated at $10,000.||Apart from Ngulu, the only wind information available is from Yap, where the airport recorded a maximum gust of 39 mph at 0608 GST on the 30th.

After developing about 250 miles north of Pohnpei on September 29th, Tropical Depression 20W was upgraded to Typhoon Melor on October 1st about 550 miles east of Guam. Melor moved west-northwest and intensified further over the next two days, passing just north of Anatahan in the northern Mariana Islands the afternoon and evening of the 3rd with maximum sustained winds of 130 mph. Typhoon Melor brought tropical storm conditions to Saipan and Agrihan during its passage.||On Saipan, Melor's closest approach was 90 miles north of the island at 1900 GST. The maximum sustained wind at the airport was from the west at 36 mph. The time is unknown, but was likely during the late afternoon prior to closest approach. The peak gust of 53 mph was from the west-southwest at 1838 GST. However, reports from the north end of Saipan indicate that winds there were stronger, with sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts of 60 mph or more likely. the minimum SLP of 995.6 mb was reported at 1854 GST. Rainfall at the airport was 2.53 inches on the 3rd. ||At Tinian, winds remained below tropical storm levels, but the automated station recorded 3.25 inches of rain on the 3rd. ||Other than a few trees blown over, no damage was reported on Tinian. Saipan reported only minor damage to its power network, with about 500 people losing power during the storm. Power was fully restored by October 6th. No injuries were reported on either island. In addition, no reports concerning injuries or damage were received from Agrihan. Total damage is estimated at $5000.

On October 15th, the automated station at Apra harbor, Guam, reported gusts of 59 mph from the east in the hour ending at 0751 GST, and 61 mph from the east-southeast in the hour ending at 1151 GST. These gusts were produced by brief lines of showers associated with the passage of minimal Tropical Storm Lupit well south of Guam. No damage was reported.
